content:
6 min read 1 hour ago -- Two lightweight React utilities born from problems I kept solving in every project. Press enter or click to view image in full size Photo by Rahul Mishra on UnsplashMost developers have those small problems that keep showing up from project to project. They’re usually not difficult enough to deserve a full library, but they’re annoying enough that you end up rewriting the same solution every time. While working on several React projects, I realized I had two of those problems. The first was tracking when every image inside a container had finished loading. Some parts of my UI — such as transitions, animations, or simply revealing content — needed to wait until all images were ready before they could run. The second was conditional rendering. Depending on the situation, I would end up using a rendering function, object mapping, or a series of conditional components like Chakra UI’s Show. None of these approaches were wrong, but I kept finding situations where they either interrupted the flow of the component or became harder to read as the JSX grew. Eventually, I decided to build two small packages for myself. They solved the problems the way I wanted to write React code, and I decided to publish them. 1. use-images-tracker This package came from a very simple frustration. Whenever I needed to know whether every image inside a particular element had finished loading, I found myself writing nearly the same logic again. Sometimes it was a helper function, sometimes it lived inside a useEffect, and sometimes it was copied from another project with a few modifications. At some point, it became obvious that this should simply be a reusable hook. The original idea was inspired by this Stack Overflow answer. I adapted the idea into a React hook and wrapped everything into a package. Installation npm install use-images-trackerBasic usage Create the hook: const { tracker, isLoaded, failures, repeating, } = useImagesTracker();Attach the tracker ref to the container whose images you want to monitor:
...
Once every image inside that container has either loaded or failed, the hook updates its state, allowing you to react accordingly. For example: useEffect(() => { if (isLoaded) { console.log("Images are loaded"); } }, [isLoaded]);This makes it easy to delay animations, remove loading placeholders, or perform any action that depends on all images being processed. Handling failed images The hook also exposes a failures array. Each item represents an image that couldn’t be loaded and contains the following information: • image — the element that failed. • code — the HTTP status code, such as 404 or 403, whenever it's available. • url — the image URL. This can be useful for debugging, logging, or replacing missing images with fallbacks. Checking again Sometimes the content inside the container changes after the initial render — for example, after fetching new data. Instead of recreating the hook, you can simply call: repeating();This resets isLoaded to false and starts tracking the images again. It ended up being one of those utilities that doesn’t do anything particularly complex, but it saves me from rewriting the same logic every time I need it. package page on npm. 2. react-switch-component The second package came from a different kind of problem. React already gives us several good ways to handle conditional rendering. Depending on the situation, I would usually reach for one of them. The problem wasn’t that these patterns were bad — it was that none of them felt equally comfortable in every scenario. One of the most common approaches is object mapping. For example: const PAGE = { home: Home, about: About, contact: Contact, };Then inside the component: const [page, setPage] = useState("home");const Page = PAGE[page] || NotFound; And finally:
This pattern is clean, simple, and works great when you’re switching between complete components. Another common approach is using a function for rendering: function renderPage() { switch (page) { case "home": return ; case "about": return ; case "contact": return ; default: return ; } } Which is then used like this:
{renderPage()}
This is also a perfectly valid solution and is often the most straightforward option. Some UI libraries even provide dedicated components for conditional rendering. For example, Chakra UI includes the Show component: Again, there’s nothing wrong with this approach. In fact, I used it quite a bit while working with Chakra UI. The issue I kept running into wasn’t functionality — it was readability. With object mapping, I often had to jump outside the JSX to understand what was actually being rendered. With a rendering function, I had to move back and forth between the JSX and another part of the component. With multiple Show components, the JSX sometimes became visually repetitive, especially when the conditions started growing. None of these are major problems, but they were enough to interrupt the reading flow while working on larger components. I wanted something that kept the rendering logic exactly where it was being used while still reading naturally. That eventually led me to build react-switch-component. The API is inspired by JavaScript’s switch statement while keeping everything inside JSX. Installation npm install @hbi-developer/react-switch-componentBasic usage
The goal isn’t to replace existing patterns. It’s simply another option for developers who prefer keeping conditional rendering directly inside the JSX while making the structure easy to scan from top to bottom. Wrapper layout One feature I found surprisingly useful is the ability to wrap every rendered case with a shared layout. There are situations where every possible branch needs to live inside the same wrapper — for styling, layout, refs, or because a UI library expects a specific parent component. Instead of repeating that wrapper for every case, you can define it once:
The package also supports delayed switching between rendered components, which can be useful for transitions or coordinating UI changes. Rather than filling this article with every available feature, I’ll leave those details to the documentation, where each one is explained with examples. package page on npm. Final thoughts I’ve been using both of these packages in my own projects whenever these situations come up, and they’ve saved me from rewriting the same pieces of logic over and over again. Neither package is trying to reinvent React or replace existing patterns. React already provides plenty of good solutions, and which one feels best often depends on the project and the developer. These packages simply reflect the way I prefer to structure my code. I found them useful enough to keep using them across different projects, so I decided to publish them in case someone else finds them useful too. If you’re interested in trying them out, I’d be happy to hear how they fit into your workflow — or where they don’t. Suggestions, bug reports, feature requests, and even alternative approaches are all welcome. After all, one of the things I enjoy most about the React ecosystem is that there are usually multiple good ways to solve the same problem. Sometimes a small utility is all it takes to make one of those approaches feel a little more natural. If you decide to give either package a try, I hope it saves you a bit of time. Happy coding.
